That would naturally put upward pressure on prices at some point. But we also have the fact that demand is indeed expanding.

From 2016 through 2025, global demand for silver ended up jumping 15.6%. Now, to be fair, we have actually seen a drop over the last four years amounting to 10.6%. However, certain categories of demand continue to show strength. From 2016 through 2025, for instance, the amount of silver used in photovoltaics that are necessary for solar power skyrocketed 139.8%. That has taken it up from accounting for only 8.2% of global demand to being responsible for consuming 17% of it. There have been other changes as well. But overwhelmingly, photovoltaics and electrical and electronic products more broadly, have been problematic for it.

Those who are bullish about silver will almost certainly point toward the expectation of a continued expansion in the solar industry. Back in 2010, global photovoltaic energy capacity amounted to just 40GW. That number ballooned to 1TW by 2022 before doubling by 2024. Estimates vary, but as we continue to shift toward a carbon-neutral world, the amount of capacity needed for solar power will expand to between 18TW and 63TW by the year 2050. One estimate said that, by that time, solar power will account for 50% of global electricity generation. That means that we would have to see capacity of about 12.5TW, though it did also leave open the door for additional growth beyond this base case scenario.

Although the data is a bit outdated, we can see in the chart below projections for solar demand from 2010 through 2027. This is specifically the share of cumulative power capacity that was projected when this analysis was conducted back in 2023. By 2027, it was estimated that about 22.2% of power capacity globally would be accounted for using solar energy. This is thanks to significant investments being made by not only the US, but by China, India, and the European Union.

We also have another catalyst that is almost certainly responsible for a lot of the move higher that silver prices have endured. This would be concern about the global economic environment. The US has become increasingly unstable from a geopolitical standpoint. The most recent government spending bills, and I mean recent as in over the last couple of administrations, have pointed toward a growth in debt related spending. So when you add together economic uncertainty with geopolitical risk, there is naturally going to be a flight to safety. Gold has historically been that main source outside of the US dollar. But with gold prices having skyrocketed over the last couple of years now, it makes sense that investors and speculators alike would buy more heavily into silver than they have in the past. Although silver consumption as a means of physical investment is lower than it was during the COVID-19 pandemic, it is still 13.2% above what it was for the average of the four years ending in 2019.

In February of this year, The Silver Institute provided their own assessment of what is going on right now and what will occur throughout 2026. They unfortunately do project that this year will be the 6th consecutive annual market deficit that silver will experience. In addition to citing geopolitical and U.S. policy uncertainty, they also mentioned that worries over the independence of the Federal Reserve have been a driver of increased demand for silver. The expectation is that while demand this year will be pretty much flat compared to last year, that will only be because of steep declines in certain categories like in jewelry and silverware. There is expected to be a 2% drop in demand when it comes to industrial fabrication. And a lot of that can be chalked up to near term pain for the photovoltaic sector as the rise in global solar installations will be dealt with through thrifting and substitution away from silver that will see overall demand for silver decline even as solar investment ramps up. Supply, meanwhile, is expected to rise by about 1.5%. This is thanks in part to the thought that silver mine production will grow by 1% thanks to recently commissioned projects such as those in Mexico and China.

In the near term, this could certainly push prices lower. But it is my own personal belief that worsening economic conditions in the US as we barrel toward a recession, as well as heightened political uncertainty, will likely offset this downward pressure. Long term, however, we will see certain challenges facing the space. This is thanks in large part to increased substitution that will make silver less prevalent as a component in photovoltaics.

Copper is one interesting alternative. It is cheap, and it is estimated to be around 94% as conductive as silver is. This is not to say that it is perfect. There are certain limitations, such as the risk of corrosion and degradation. But that doesn't mean that investments aren't being made in this regard. In January of this year, for instance, Chinese module manufacturer Longi revealed that it will begin mass production of base metal photovoltaic cells in the second quarter of this year, essentially transitioning toward copper based metallization because of rising silver prices and cost pressures. It's not the only company moving that way. In fact, according to the aforementioned source, it's believed that there will be a 7% drop in demand for silver specifically when it comes to photovoltaic modules this year.

There are other examples of a shift away from silver. New designs of photovoltaic cells that utilize more efficient production methods have allowed a continuous decline in silver grams for each watt. And investments in silver free architectures, though still early stage, offer promise. One example of this can be seen by looking at heterojunction solar cell screens that are printed using silver copper on one side and silver paste on another in a manner that captures additional efficiencies while reducing the reliance on silver as a standalone metal. With costs as high as they are right now, it is likely that investments are going to increase in areas such as this.

Takeaway

To be honest with you, the only reason I am not bearish on silver this moment is because of the political and economic climate that we find ourselves in. There are certain structural issues as I have detailed already that are problematic for silver demand moving forward. We could see some weakness in certain areas this year, such as those involving photovoltaic cells. But with demand still outpacing supply this year and worries about geopolitical and economic conditions, I think that the near term picture will be supportive of these elevated prices. That's why, for now, I think that a neutral stance on silver is justified. But in the event that we start to see the economic and political climate stabilize, I would turn bearish on it very quickly.

data.title: Lord Abbett Total Return Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • The first quarter was defined by a balance between economic resilience and rising geopolitical and macro uncertainty. Market performance faced headwinds from weakness in Technology/Software (driven by AI disruption concerns) and escalating geopolitical tensions, though supportive macroeconomic conditions helped offset some of the pressure. Labor market signals were mixed and inflation remained above target, but expectations for earnings growth, fiscal stimulus, and eventual tariff stabilization helped anchor the outlook. Late in the quarter, geopolitical risks intensified as tensions in the Middle East disrupted energy markets and pushed oil prices higher, delaying expectations for rate cuts and introducing near-term inflation risks. Despite this backdrop, the underlying economy remained constructive, supported by solid consumer spending, continued earnings momentum, and expectations for longer-term policy support.
  • Over the quarter, investment grade corporate 2 credit spreads widened by 11 basis points (bps), commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) 3 spreads tightened by 1 bp, and asset-backed securities (ABS) 4 spreads were flat. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.48% to 3.79% and the 10-Year U.S. Treasury yield moved higher from 4.17% to 4.32% 1 .

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned -0.28%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended March 31 st , 2026. The Fund’s benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index*, returned -0.05% during the same period.
  • The portfolio’s rates positioning was the primary detractor from relative performance over the quarter. In particular, the portfolio’s modestly longer duration detracted from relative performance as rates moved higher over the quarter.
  • Security selection within investment grade corporate bonds also detracted from relative performance, particularly within the Communications and Technology sectors. Over the quarter, we decreased the portfolio’s allocation to investment grade corporate bonds, particularly in sectors more vulnerable to AI disruptions.
  • An allocation to non-agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) was the primary contributor to relative performance. Over the quarter, we increased the portfolio’s exposure to non-agency MBS, primarily through AAA-rated non-qualified mortgage securities where we see attractive relative value.
  • Security selection within agency mortgage-backed securities also contributed to relative performance. We increased exposure to agency MBS during the period given the sector’s high quality and liquidity amid the market volatility.
